agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform adding real-time driver earnings breakdown (base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, deductions) across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different labor classifications, legal requirements, and institutional approval processes.",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Compensation & Earnings Visibility",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The guaranteed compensation a driver receives for accepting and completing a delivery, independent of tips or bonuses. For employees (CA), this must meet or exceed minimum wage on a per-hour basis. For contractors (Indonesia), this is the agreed rate per task." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation awarded on top of base pay for each completed delivery, potentially influenced by distance, time-of-day, demand surge, or performance metrics." },
      { "term": "Tip", "definition": "Voluntary gratuity from the customer to the driver. Must never be commingled with or offset against base pay or bonuses. Platform must pass through 100% of tips to the driver." },
      { "term": "Deduction", "definition": "Any amount subtracted from a driver's gross earnings, such as equipment fees, insurance contributions, tax withholdings, or platform fees. Each deduction must be individually itemized with a reason." },
      { "term": "Earnings Breakdown", "definition": "A structured, itemized view of a driver's compensation for a given period, decomposed into base pay, bonuses, tips, and deductions, with a resulting net earnings figure." },
      { "term": "Driver Classification", "definition": "The legal labor status of a driver — employee or independent contractor — which determines applicable wage laws, tax treatment, benefit obligations, and transparency requirements." },
      { "term": "Works Council Approval", "definition": "In Germany, the legally required consent from the Betriebsrat before any system that monitors, evaluates, or displays employee compensation data can be deployed." },
      { "term": "Net Earnings", "definition": "Gross earnings (base pay + bonuses + tips) minus all deductions. The final amount the driver receives or is owed." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Tips must never be used to offset or subsidize base pay or per-delivery bonuses. They are additive and must be passed through to the driver at 100%.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Tip pass-through is both a legal requirement (California Labor Code) and a fundamental trust contract with drivers. Violating this corrupts the compensation model and creates legal liability in all jurisdictions.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any earnings period, net_earnings >= base_pay + bonuses - deductions, AND tips_displayed == tips_received_from_customers with zero platform retention."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Every deduction must be individually itemized with a human-readable reason. No 'miscellaneous' or aggregate deduction line items are permitted.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "An opaque deduction violates the core purpose of transparency and is legally non-compliant in California. A deduction without a reason is corrupt data.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every deduction line item, a non-empty deduction_reason string must be present, and no single line item may aggregate multiple distinct deduction types."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "In California, the earnings breakdown must be displayed to drivers and must include all components (base pay, bonuses, tips, deductions, net earnings). This is not optional — wage transparency is legally mandated for employees under AB5.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California labor law (AB5, wage transparency statutes) legally requires itemized wage statements for employees. Non-compliance exposes the platform to regulatory penalties and class-action liability.",
      "testable_assertion": "When driver.jurisdiction == 'CA_USA' and driver.classification == 'employee', the earnings breakdown response must contain non-null values for base_pay, bonuses, tips, deductions, and net_earnings."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "In Germany, the earnings transparency feature must NOT be deployed until works council (Betriebsrat) approval is obtained and recorded in the system. The feature must be gated behind this approval status.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "German Works Constitution Act (Betriebsverfassungsgesetz §87) gives the works council co-determination rights over systems that monitor or display employee performance and compensation data. Deploying without approval is illegal and can result in injunctions.",
      "testable_assertion": "When driver.jurisdiction == 'DE' AND works_council_approval_status != 'approved', the earnings breakdown feature must return a 'feature_not_available' response and must not display any compensation data."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Earnings breakdown must reflect real-time or near-real-time data. 'Real-time' means data no older than the completion of the most recent delivery in the current active session.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The freshness window is a product policy decision. A VP could decide 15-minute delay is acceptable. However, showing stale data undermines trust.",
      "testable_assertion": "After a delivery is marked 'completed', the earnings breakdown must include that delivery's earnings components within 60 seconds."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Net earnings must always equal the arithmetic sum: base_pay + total_bonuses + total_tips - total_deductions. No rounding errors, hidden fees, or unaccounted discrepancies.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If net earnings doesn't reconcile with its components, the system is producing corrupt financial data. This is a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every earnings breakdown: abs(net_earnings - (base_pay + total_bonuses + total_tips - total_deductions)) == 0 (using decimal arithmetic, not floating point)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Driver classification (employee vs. independent contractor) must be sourced from the driver's jurisdiction-specific legal status, not inferred or defaulted. Classification determines which earnings rules and display requirements apply.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Misclassification is a legal violation that compounds — wrong classification leads to wrong tax treatment, wrong deductions, wrong transparency rules. The system is in a corrupt state if classification is wrong.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every driver record must have a non-null classification field that is set based on jurisdiction-specific rules, and no default/fallback classification is permitted."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "In Indonesia, displaying the earnings breakdown is permitted but not legally required. The feature may be offered as opt-in or default-on, per product policy.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a product/policy decision since no legal mandate exists. The business can choose the default behavior.",
      "testable_assertion": "When driver.jurisdiction == 'ID' and driver.classification == 'independent_contractor', the earnings breakdown feature is available but its visibility may be toggled by product configuration."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "Tax withholdings for employees (CA, DE) must appear as explicit deduction line items. For independent contractors (Indonesia), tax responsibility lies with the driver, and the platform must not display withholdings it is not making.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying tax withholdings that aren't being withheld, or hiding withholdings that are, produces materially false financial information.",
      "testable_assertion": "When driver.classification == 'employee', deductions must include tax withholding line items. When driver.classification == 'independent_contractor', no tax withholding line items appear unless the platform actually withholds taxes."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver in California completes a delivery",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see itemized base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, all deductions with reasons, tax withholdings, and net earnings — all fields are non-null and the net earnings reconcile exactly"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver in Germany where works council approval has NOT been granted",
      "when": "The driver attempts to view the earnings breakdown",
      "then": "The feature is unavailable and no compensation data is displayed; the driver sees a message explaining the feature is pending approval"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver in Germany where works council approval HAS been granted",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see the full itemized earnings breakdown, equivalent to the California experience, including all deductions and tax withholdings"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver in Indonesia completes a delivery",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, platform deductions (if any) with reasons, and net earnings — but no tax withholding lines (since they are independent contractors and the platform does not withhold taxes)"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A customer tips a driver $10 on a delivery",
      "when": "The driver views the earnings breakdown for that delivery",
      "then": "The tip line item shows exactly $10 (or local currency equivalent) with no platform deduction from the tip amount"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A driver has multiple deductions applied (equipment rental, insurance, platform fee)",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "Each deduction appears as a separate line item with its own reason — no aggregated 'Other Deductions' line"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A driver completes a delivery",
      "when": "They check their earnings breakdown within 60 seconds of delivery completion",
      "then": "The completed delivery's earnings components are reflected in the breakdown"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A new driver is onboarded without a classification being set",
      "when": "The system attempts to generate an earnings breakdown",
      "then": "The system rejects the request and raises an error — it does not default to any classification or display partial data"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A delivery is completed but the tip is added by the customer after the driver has already viewed the breakdown", "expected_behavior": "The earnings breakdown must update to reflect the tip once it is recorded. Previously viewed breakdowns must not be treated as immutable — the driver must see the updated total on next view." },
    { "scenario": "A deduction results in net earnings below zero for a period", "expected_behavior": "The system must display the negative net earnings accurately. It must not clamp to zero or hide the deficit. For employees (CA), this may indicate an illegal deduction that brings earnings below minimum wage — flag for compliance review." },
    { "scenario": "Works council approval in Germany is revoked after initial deployment", "expected_behavior": "The feature must be immediately disabled for German drivers. The gate must check approval status dynamically, not just at deployment time." },
    { "scenario": "A driver operates across jurisdictions (e.g., lives in Germany but makes a delivery in a border region)", "expected_behavior": "Earnings rules and display requirements must be determined by the driver's employment jurisdiction, not the delivery location. Escalate to legal if cross-border scenarios are common." },
    { "scenario": "Currency conversion for tips — customer pays in one currency, driver is paid in another", "expected_behavior": "The tip amount displayed must be in the driver's payout currency, with the conversion rate and original amount visible for transparency." },
    { "scenario": "Platform outage causes earnings data to be temporarily unavailable", "expected_behavior": "The system must clearly indicate that data is temporarily unavailable rather than showing stale or partial data without a staleness indicator. For California, a prolonged outage may require fallback compliance measures." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is cancelled after the driver has started — partial base pay applies", "expected_behavior": "The earnings breakdown must show the partial base pay with a clear reason (e.g., 'cancelled delivery — partial pay') and not omit the cancelled delivery from the record." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Correct implementation protects against significant legal exposure: California wage-statement violations carry penalties of $50-$100 per pay period per employee (PAGA claims can scale to millions). German works council violations can result in injunctions halting operations. Tip transparency builds driver trust, reducing churn — driver acquisition costs in logistics typically run $300-$1,500 per driver.",
    "operational": "Requires jurisdiction-aware feature gating infrastructure. Germany's works council approval creates a hard external dependency on the rollout timeline that engineering cannot control. Real-time earnings calculation adds load to the compensation service. Must coordinate with payroll, tax, and compliance teams across three jurisdictions.",
    "user": "Drivers gain trust and financial clarity, directly correlating with retention and satisfaction. Opaque pay is the #1 driver complaint in gig logistics — transparency is a competitive differentiator. However, if deductions are poorly explained or surprising, transparency can backfire and increase support volume.",
    "risk": "HIGH legal risk if California compliance is incomplete (class-action exposure). HIGH operational risk if Germany's works council gate is bypassed (injunction risk). MEDIUM risk of driver dissatisfaction if deductions are shown without adequate context. LOW risk in Indonesia due to absence of legal mandate, but reputational risk if transparency is perceived as inferior compared to other markets.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers (California)", "impact": "Legal entitlement to itemized wage statements fulfilled. Builds trust. Incorrect implementation creates grounds for individual and class-action claims." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Germany)", "impact": "Feature availability is blocked on works council. If deployed prematurely, drivers AND works council could file complaints. Once approved, provides same transparency benefits." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Indonesia)", "impact": "Positive but optional — increased trust and competitive advantage in local market. No legal downside." },
      { "group": "Legal & Compliance Team", "impact": "Must validate earnings display meets California Labor Code §226 requirements and German Betriebsverfassungsgesetz §87. Ongoing compliance monitoring required." },
      { "group": "Works Council (Germany)", "impact": "Co-determination right triggered. Must be engaged early. Failure to consult is a legal violation independent of the feature's merits." },
      { "group": "Finance / Payroll", "impact": "Must ensure earnings breakdown data is consistent with actual payroll calculations. Any discrepancy between displayed and paid amounts creates legal and trust issues." },
      { "group": "Product Team", "impact": "Must design jurisdiction-aware UX that handles feature gating (Germany), mandatory display (California), and optional display (Indonesia) without three entirely separate implementations." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Expect increase in driver inquiries about deductions once made visible. Support teams need training on earnings breakdown explanations." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 45,
    "reasoning": "The spec is a single sentence that spans three jurisdictions with fundamentally different legal frameworks, labor classifications, and institutional requirements. Critical details are unspecified: what specific deductions exist, how tips are collected and passed through, whether the platform actually withholds taxes in each jurisdiction, what the works council engagement timeline looks like, and whether cross-border scenarios exist. The legal and regulatory dimensions require legal review that has not been confirmed. The Germany works council requirement creates a hard blocker with an undefined timeline.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Tip pass-through must be 100% — this is universal and well-established in logistics",
      "California requires itemized wage statements for employees — this is settled law",
      "Germany requires works council approval for compensation display systems — this is settled law",
      "Net earnings must reconcile exactly with component sum — basic financial integrity",
      "Driver classification must be explicit, never defaulted"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact California Labor Code §226 requirements for digital wage statements — need legal review",
      "Whether German works council approval is per-feature or per-system — need legal counsel",
      "Indonesia-specific regulations on platform fee transparency for contractors — local law review needed",
      "Tax withholding specifics per jurisdiction — depends on platform's actual tax obligations",
      "Whether 'real-time' has a legally defined meaning in California wage contexts",
      "Cross-border driver scenarios and which jurisdiction's rules apply"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform withholds taxes for employees (CA, DE) but not for contractors (ID)",
      "Works council approval is required per-feature, not already covered by a blanket existing approval",
      "Tips are collected by the platform and passed through — not paid directly driver-to-customer",
      "Each driver belongs to exactly one jurisdiction for classification purposes",
      "The platform uses decimal arithmetic for financial calculations, not floating point"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The spec does not define what deductions exist on the platform, nor how they vary by jurisdiction. Without knowing the deduction types, we cannot validate whether the earnings breakdown covers all cases or whether certain deductions might be illegal in specific jurisdictions (e.g., deductions that bring California employees below minimum wage are prohibited).",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Legal & Compliance", "Finance / Payroll"],
      "options": ["Enumerate all current deduction types across jurisdictions before implementation", "Build a generic deduction model and validate per-jurisdiction legality separately"],
      "recommendation": "Enumerate all deduction types now. A generic model risks encoding illegal deductions without catching them."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Germany's works council engagement status and timeline are unknown. This is a hard legal prerequisite — the feature cannot launch in Germany without it. Engineering must not build Germany-specific features without knowing when (or if) approval will be granted.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Works Council (Germany)", "Drivers (Germany)", "Legal & Compliance", "Product Team"],
      "options": ["Begin works council engagement immediately and build the feature with a Germany gate", "Exclude Germany from initial rollout entirely"],
      "recommendation": "Build with a jurisdiction-level feature gate from day one. Begin works council engagement in parallel. Do not skip the gate even for testing."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec says 'real-time' but does not define an SLA. In California, wage statement timeliness may have legal implications. Does real-time mean per-delivery, per-session, per-day? What happens during system outages — is there a compliance fallback?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Legal & Compliance", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["Define real-time as 'within 60 seconds of delivery completion'", "Define real-time as 'on next app refresh after delivery completion'", "Consult California labor law for any timeliness requirements on digital wage statements"],
      "recommendation": "Define as 'within 60 seconds of delivery completion' for product purposes, but consult legal on whether California imposes additional timeliness requirements for wage statement availability."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No legal review has been confirmed for any of the three jurisdictions. California Labor Code §226 has specific requirements for itemized wage statements (format, retention, employee access rights). German and Indonesian labor laws may have additional requirements not captured here.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal & Compliance", "All driver groups"],
      "options": ["Proceed with domain analysis and flag for legal review before implementation", "Block all implementation until legal review is complete"],
      "recommendation": "Do not ship to any jurisdiction without jurisdiction-specific legal sign-off. Domain analysis can proceed, but acceptance criteria must be validated by legal counsel before being treated as final."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
